mission.title: Install and Configure Helm on Kubernetes
mission.description: Helm is the Kubernetes Package Manager that simplifies the process of managing Kubernetes applications through the use of Charts. Installing Helm allows you to easily deploy, manage, and share applications on your Kubernetes cluster.

steps.title: Download the Helm binary
steps.description:
Download the Helm v4.1.1 binary and add it to your PATH. Use the following commands:
```bash
wget https://get.helm.sh/helm-v4.1.1-linux-amd64.tar.gz
 tar -zxvf helm-v4.1.1-linux-amd64.tar.gz
 sudo mv linux-amd64/helm /usr/local/bin/helm
```
Make sure to replace 'linux-amd64' with the appropriate directory for your OS if you are using a different platform.

steps.title: Verify Helm installation
steps.description:
Check that Helm is installed correctly by running:
```bash
helm version
```

steps.title: Add the Helm stable repository
steps.description:
Add the official Helm stable repository to your Helm configuration:
```bash
helm repo add stable https://charts.helm.sh/stable
```

steps.title: Update Helm repositories
steps.description:
Update your local Helm chart repository cache:
```bash
helm repo update
```

steps.title: Install a sample Helm chart
steps.description:
Install a sample application using Helm, such as Nginx:
```bash
helm install my-nginx stable/nginx --namespace default --create-namespace
```

steps.title: Verify the installation
steps.description:
Check that the Nginx pod is running:
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace default
```

resolution.summary: A successful installation will have Helm installed and configured, with the Nginx application deployed in your Kubernetes cluster. You can verify the deployment by checking the status of the Nginx pod.
codeSnippets:
wget https://get.helm.sh/helm-v4.1.1-linux-amd64.tar.gz
 tar -zxvf helm-v4.1.1-linux-amd64.tar.gz
 sudo mv linux-amd64/helm /usr/local/bin/helm

uninstall.title: Remove the Helm release
uninstall.description:
Uninstall the Helm release to remove the deployed application from your cluster. Use the following command to delete the Nginx release:
```bash
helm uninstall my-nginx --namespace default
```

uninstall.title: Clean up CRDs and persistent resources
uninstall.description:
If there are any Custom Resource Definitions (CRDs) or persistent volume claims (PVCs) associated with the release, clean them up. Use the following commands to delete the CRDs and PVCs:
```bash
kubectl delete pvc --all --namespace default
kubectl delete crd nginxes.example.com
```

uninstall.title: Remove namespace and verify
uninstall.description:
If the namespace was created specifically for this release and is no longer needed, delete the namespace. Verify that it has been removed:
```bash
kubectl delete namespace default
kubectl get namespaces
```

upgrade.title: Backup current state
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, it is important to back up the current state of the release. You can do this by exporting the current configuration:
```bash
helm get values my-nginx --namespace default > my-nginx-backup.yaml
```

upgrade.title: Update repo and run upgrade command
upgrade.description:
Update the Helm repository and then run the upgrade command to upgrade the release to the latest version:
```bash
helm repo update
helm upgrade my-nginx stable/nginx --namespace default
```

upgrade.title: Verify the upgrade
upgrade.description:
After the upgrade, verify that the release has been upgraded successfully and the pods are running:
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace default
helm list --namespace default
```

troubleshooting.title: Pods stuck in CrashLoopBackOff
troubleshooting.description:
If your Nginx pods are in a CrashLoopBackOff state, check the logs to diagnose the issue:
```bash
kubectl logs <pod-name> --namespace default
``` 
Common causes include misconfigurations or missing environment variables.

troubleshooting.title: Helm release not found
troubleshooting.description:
If you encounter an error stating that the Helm release is not found, ensure you are using the correct namespace:
```bash
helm list --namespace default
``` 
If the release does not appear, it may have been uninstalled or created in a different namespace.

troubleshooting.title: Insufficient permissions
troubleshooting.description:
If you receive permission errors when trying to install or upgrade, ensure that your Kubernetes context has the necessary permissions. Check your current context:
```bash
kubectl config current-context
``` 
You may need to switch contexts or update your RBAC settings.

troubleshooting.title: Chart version compatibility issues
troubleshooting.description:
If you encounter issues related to chart versions, verify that the version of the chart you are trying to install is compatible with your Kubernetes version:
```bash
helm search repo stable/nginx --versions
``` 
Consider specifying a compatible version in your install or upgrade command.

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have a Kubernetes cluster running and kubectl configured to interact with it.
